title: -Surveillance Technology Ordinance Submission for Berkeley City Jail Surveillance System

sections.recommendation:
Adopt a Resolution:
1. Accepting the attached Surveillance Acquisition Report for the Berkeley
City Jail Surveillance System, pursuant to Berkeley Municipal Code
(B.M.C.) Chapter 2.99.
2. Accepting the attached Surveillance Use Policy: Use of Internal Video
Surveillance System (BPD Policy 1306)
sections.current_situation:
The internal surveillance camera system that is the subject of this request is part of a
larger Public Safety Building (PSB) Detention Area Security System Renovation Project.
The Berkeley Police Department, with support from the Public Works Department, is
currently in the bidding phase of a project to replace the jail control panel and to
upgrade and expand the surveillance camera system. Portions of this project are
contingent upon City Council approval of both a Surveillance Acquisition Report and a
Surveillance Use Policy, as required by Berkeley Municipal Code (BMC) Chapter 2.99.
The attached resolution is limited to satisfying the requirements of BMC 2.99 by
approving the Surveillance Acquisition Report and Surveillance Use Policy.
sections.background:
The Berkeley City Jail, located at 2100 Martin Luther King Jr. Way, was constructed in
2000. The jail is secured using a combination of a control panel, which controls the
opening and closing of various entry/exit doors and cell doors, and a surveillance
camera system. An operator in the control room monitors the safety of employees and
detainees using this control panel system and the surveillance camera system. The
current control panel system is 25 years-old, has exceeded its expected service life, and
is now obsolete. Similarly, the surveillance camera system is composed of outdated
analog and digital cameras, some over two decades old, with many areas of the jail
lacking camera coverage. As part of a comprehensive redesign of the jail surveillance
system, both the control panel and surveillance camera system require replacement.
2180 Milvia Street, Berkeley, CA 94704 ● Tel: (510) 981-7000 ● TDD: (510) 981-6903 ● Fax: (510) 981-7099
E-Mail: manager@CityofBerkeley.info Website: http://www.CityofBerkeley.info/Manager
Page 2 of 12
Surveillance Technology Ordinance Berkeley City Jail Surveillance System CONSENT CALENDAR
July 22, 2025
The planned expansion will provide full recorded video coverage of the facility and
integrate directly with the new control panel system. This resolution is intended to
secure the required approvals under BMC 2.99, the City’s Surveillance Technology
Ordinance.
ENVIRONMENTAL SUSTAINABILITY AND CLIMATE IMPACTS
There are no identified environmental or climate impacts associated with the proposed
project.
sections.fiscal_impacts:
This project was anticipated to originally cost $500,000 and resources were made
available to the Public Works Department in the FY2023 Capital Budget. However, due
to extended project timelines, in order to implement the surveillance camera system, an
additional $250,000 is needed. Given the structural deficit in the General Fund, these
resources are not immediately available and the Police Department will need to secure
these funds before moving to the construction phase. Public Works will be bringing an
item to Council to contract with a vendor following the Request for Proposal process.
sections.rationale:
This Resolution will allow the Police Department to proceed with replacing the jail
control panel and surveillance camera system. Integration of the upgraded surveillance
camera system into the new control panel will enhance safety for both employees and
detainees and ensure operational reliability within the Berkeley Jail facility.
sections.alternatives:
An alternative to approve the project would be to delay or forgo the replacement of the
jail control panel and surveillance camera system. However, this option is not
recommended due to significant operational and safety concerns. The current
surveillance camera system lacks comprehensive coverage and relies on obsolete
technology, limiting the Department’s ability to effectively monitor detainee movement,
respond to incidents, and ensure accountability. Gaps in video coverage may also
expose the City to liability in the event of disputes or critical incidents. Delaying this
project would prevent integration of modern surveillance capabilities into the new control
panel system, undermining operational efficiency and delaying much needed
infrastructure upgrades for the Berkeley Jail.
CONTACT PERSON
Chief Jennifer Louis (510) 981-5700

the Berkeley City Jail Surveillance System

RESOLUTION NO. ##,###-N.S.
APPROVING SURVEILLANCE ACQUISITION REPORT AND SURVEILLANCE USE
POLICY FOR THE BERKELEY CITY JAIL SURVEILLANCE SYSTEM
WHEREAS, on March 13, 2018, City Council adopted Ordinance NO. 7,592-N.S., the
Surveillance Technology Use and Community Safety Ordinance; and
WHEREAS, the Berkeley City Jail has been in operation at 2100 Martin Luther King Junior
Blvd. since 2000; and
WHEREAS, Renovation and redesign of the Jail Control Panel and Surveillance System
in the Jail has become necessary due to obsolescence of the existing system; and
WHEREAS, the Berkeley Police Department in collaboration with City of Berkeley Public
Works undertook a PSB Detention Area Security System Renovation Project; and
WHEREAS, the Surveillance Technology Use and Community Safety Ordinance requires
Council review and approval of a Surveillance Acquisition Report describing the proposed
surveillance technology; and
WHEREAS, the Surveillance Technology Use and Community Safety Ordinance requires
Council review and approval of a Surveillance Use Policy for the proposed surveillance
technology; and
WHEREAS, the Berkeley Police Department has submitted the required Surveillance
Acquisition Report and Surveillance Use Policy required by the Ordinance; and
WHEREAS, $500,000 has been transferred to Public Works 501 Capital Improvement
Fund in support of the PSB Detention Area Security System Renovation Project; and
WHEREAS, additional funding will be requested in the FY 2026 budget process; and
WHEREAS, the Council reviewed these documents has considered and balanced the
City’s interest in protecting public safety with its interest in protecting the privacy and civil
